planting.note: Plant dormant crowns or divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; wavy-leaved hosta is one of the classic variegated hostas, with creamy-white centers and twisted, undulating leaves that make it a visual standout even in deep shade.

directions: Plant dormant crowns 1–2 inches deep in spring in moist, organically rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; keep consistently moist but never waterlogged. Hosta undulata is one of the original classic variegated hostas, producing medium-sized, undulating (wavy) leaves with creamy-white to pure white centers and irregular green margins that brighten shaded borders considerably; it naturalizes reliably in deep shade where many other variegated hostas fade or revert to solid green. The distinctive twisted, rippled leaf texture gives it a dynamic quality that catches light even in dim conditions. Lavender flowers on 18–24 inch scapes in mid-summer are fragrant; divide every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor. Slug control is essential — apply iron phosphate bait or diatomaceous earth around emerging growth in early spring.

common_name: Sum and Substance Hosta (Giant Gold Hosta)

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Sum and Substance is the gold standard for giant hostas — an AHS Best of Show winner and the most widely recognized enormous hosta cultivar, with leaves that can reach 18 inches across and the entire plant reaching 4–6 feet wide.

directions: Plant in spring or early fall in moist, organically enriched, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Sum and Substance needs room — allow 4–6 feet of space for a mature plant and do not crowd it with other plants. Sum and Substance is the most famous giant hosta, an American Hosta Society Award of Merit winner with enormous, heavily textured, chartreuse-to-gold leaves up to 18 inches long and 14 inches wide on plants 24–36 inches tall and 4–6 feet across; it is the benchmark by which all large gold hostas are measured. Remarkably slug-resistant due to its thick, waxy leaf substance (the trait the name references). Golden color develops best with 3–4 hours of morning sun per day but direct afternoon sun causes scorching; partial shade with bright indirect light is ideal. Lavender flowers in late summer. Takes 3–5 years from division to reach full size.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Halcyon is the most widely admired medium blue hosta, with an intensely blue-grey leaf color that holds better through summer heat than most blue hostas and exceptional slug resistance due to thick leaf substance.

directions: Plant in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; avoid afternoon sun which fades the blue coloring and can cause leaf scorch. Halcyon is a British hybrid hosta (H. Tardiana Group) and one of the most widely grown and admired blue-grey hostas, producing medium-sized, heart-shaped, intensely glaucous blue-grey leaves with a distinctive powdery bloom that reflects light in a shaded garden; the blue coloring holds reasonably well through summer unlike many blue hostas that fade to green in heat. Lavender to violet flowers in mid-summer on 20-inch scapes are attractive. One parent of many blue Tardiana hybrids; Halcyon is considered the benchmark for this group. Very slug resistant due to thick, waxy leaves. Ideal for woodland gardens, shaded border fronts, and container growing.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; broad beech fern spreads by shallow rhizomes to form loose, airy colonies that are attractive when naturalized under deciduous trees in moist, slightly shaded woodland settings.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; broad beech fern spreads rhizomatously to naturalize into a loose, open colony under trees. Phegopteris hexagonoptera is a native deciduous fern of eastern North American moist woodlands, producing wide, triangular fronds 15–24 inches long on slender stems — the distinctive triangular shape with the lowest pair of leaflets widely angled outward ('hexagonal' refers to the six-sided shape of the frond) makes it easy to identify among other native ferns. It is delicate in appearance but robust in growth, naturalizing readily in moist, shaded settings under deciduous trees to create an airy, fine-textured ground cover. The light green, somewhat thin fronds have a fresh, springtime appearance throughout the season and die back cleanly to the ground in fall.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to dry soil in partial to full shade; Cascade Oregon grape is more shade-tolerant and lower-growing than M. aquifolium, making it a better choice for dense forest understory shade and the dry shade under conifers.

directions: Plant in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Cascade Oregon grape is more drought and shade tolerant than the better-known tall Oregon grape (M. aquifolium) and is superior for naturalizing as a ground cover in dry, shaded forest settings under conifers or other dense trees. Mahonia nervosa is a low-growing western native evergreen shrub 12–24 inches tall with long, pinnate, holly-like leaves with many narrow leaflets, clusters of yellow flowers in early spring before many other shade shrubs bloom, and blue-black berries that are loved by birds and used to make preserves and a tart beverage in the Pacific Northwest. The evergreen foliage turns wine-red to bronze in winter, providing year-round interest. An excellent, underused evergreen ground cover for the Pacific Northwest and adaptable dry-shade situations in western gardens; deer resistant.
